ideay.note: Uniquely Nica. From '¿Y de ahí?' Surprise/curiosity

chele.note: Nica slang. Not offensive. Fem: chela

pinolero.note: From pinol (corn drink). Term of pride

cuadra.note: Distances given in cuadras in Nica

nacatamal.note: Nica tamale wrapped in banana leaf

vos.note: Nica uses vos not tú. Changes conjugations

gustar.note: Me gusta = I like (it pleases me)

parece.note: ¿Qué te parece? = What do you think?

dolor.note: Tengo dolor de...=I have a pain in...

chip.note: Nica word for a phone SIM card

cuajada.note: Soft white Nica cheese, eaten with gallo pinto

fiado.note: Buying now, paying later — common at pulperías

funcionar.note: For things, not jobs. 'No funciona' = it doesn't work

gallopinto.note: Nica national dish: rice and beans fried together

maduro.note: Fried sweet plantain. Also means 'ripe' or 'mature'

refresco.note: In Nica, often fresh fruit drinks

buenas.note: Short for buenos días/buenas tardes. The everyday Nica greeting

ciento.note: Used in compound numbers: ciento treinta = 130

invierno.note: In Nica, 'invierno' means the rainy season, not cold

calor.note: 'Hace calor' = it's hot. 'Tengo calor' = I'm hot

huele.note: From oler, to smell. 'Huele a' = it smells like

muchacho.note: Friendly way to address a young person

contar.note: Both 'to tell a story' and 'to count'

encantar.note: Stronger than gustar. 'Me encanta' = I love it

estuvo.note: Past of estar. '¿Cómo estuvo?' = how was it?

platica.note: 'Platicar' = to chat. Common in Central America

acuerdo.note: 'De acuerdo' = agreed; 'estar de acuerdo' = to agree

argumento.note: A point you make, not a fight

comprender.note: A touch more formal than entender

cuenta.note: Bank account, or the check at a restaurant

deber.note: 'Te debo una' = I owe you one

formulario.note: A paper form to fill out

mantener.note: 'Mantener mi punto' = to hold my point

punto.note: 'Tener un punto' = to have a point

reclamo.note: 'Hacer un reclamo' = to make a complaint

amar.note: Deeper than querer. 'Te amo' = I love you (romantic, profound)

amor.note: 'Mi amor' = my love, a common term of endearment

apoyar.note: Emotional support; 'te apoyo' = I support you

cariño.note: Also a term of endearment, like 'dear'

hogar.note: Home as a feeling, warmer than 'casa'

ojalá.note: From Arabic. Takes the subjunctive: 'ojalá venga'

platicar.note: Common in Central America for casual talk

saudade.note: A bittersweet longing — borrowed feeling, deeply felt

sueño.note: Both a dream and sleepiness ('tengo sueño')

ahorita.note: Famously flexible — can mean immediately OR in a while. Context decides

albur.note: A pun with a hidden, often cheeky, second meaning

amanecer.en: to stay up till dawn / dawn

buena onda.note: 'Es buena onda' = he's a cool/kind person

cae bien.note: 'Me cae bien' = I like him/her (as a person)

cafecito.note: Diminutive of café — warmth, not size

captar.note: To catch a meaning or hint

carcajada.note: 'Soltar la carcajada' = to burst out laughing

chunche.note: Nica word for any object whose name you forget

doble sentido.note: A phrase with a hidden, often cheeky, second meaning

fíjate.note: 'Fíjate que...' = a classic way to start telling news

jaña.note: Casual Nica slang for a girl or girlfriend

maje.note: Like 'dude' — affectionate among friends, rude with strangers

oído.note: The trained ear for understanding speech

pillar.note: To catch on to a joke or meaning

pinche.note: In Nica: stingy or low-quality (milder than Mexican usage)

quizás.note: Often a soft 'no' in social contexts

relajo.note: 'Echar relajo' = to goof around, have fun

sano.note: Humor that's 'sano' is good-natured, not crude

vacilar.note: Central to Nica social life — playful joking

conceder.note: In debate, to grant a point

dominar.note: 'Dominar un idioma' = to master a language

habría.note: Conditional perfect: 'habría hecho' = I would have done

hubiera.note: Past subjunctive: 'si hubiera sabido' = if I had known

registro.note: Formal vs informal level of language

tuviera.note: Past subjunctive of tener: 'si tuviera' = if I had

memoria.note: Collective memory, as well as personal

moraleja.note: The lesson of a story or fable

puente.note: Often figurative: a bridge between worlds

refrán.note: A traditional fixed saying — beloved in daily speech

retruécano.note: A clever reversal of words for effect

aspirar.note: In Nica speech, the 's' is aspirated or dropped

voseo.note: The vos pronoun system, universal in Nicaragua

regalar.note: 'Regaláme' = just 'please give me', not a gift

chinear.note: To dote on or carry a child lovingly

amañarse.note: To grow attached to a place

camión.note: In Mexico = bus; in Nicaragua a bus is 'bus'

pega.note: 'Echarse un pega' = take a short nap

por ende.note: Formal connector, more refined than 'por eso'

asimismo.note: Elegant way to add a point

albergar.note: 'Albergar una esperanza' = to harbor a hope

bicho.note: Informal — a bug, or affectionately a kid
